mnemonics.title: DEF - Don't Ever Forget
mnemonics.content: To define a function in Python, always start with 'def' - Don't Ever Forget the keyword that introduces every function

mnemonics.title: PARTS - Parameters Are The Stuff
mnemonics.content: Function parameters are the inputs (Arguments) - Parameters Are The Stuff you pass in to get output

mnemonics.title: RETURN - REach EVery Time You Make a Value
mnemonics.content: Use return to send values back - REach EVery Time You Make a value you want to capture

mnemonics.title: LAMBDA - Little Abstract Making Bytecode Anew
mnemonics.content: Lambda functions are anonymous functions - Little Abstract Making Bytecode Anew (one-liner functions)

mnemonics.title: SCOPE - Stay Curiously Outside, Parameters Enter
mnemonics.content: Variables inside functions are local - Stay Curiously Outside, Parameters Enter only what's passed

mnemonics.title: DOC - Document Objects Clearly
mnemonics.content: Use docstrings to document functions - Document Objects Clearly for better code readability

acronyms.usage: Keyword used to create/define a function in Python
Variable number of positional arguments passed to a function (tuple)
Variable number of keyword arguments passed to a function (dictionary)
Statement that specifies what value a function should output
String literal that describes what a function does
Guidelines for Python code style (PEP 8 for function naming)

visualTips.visualization: Think of a function as a factory machine - you feed inputs (parameters) in one side, the machine processes them (code body), and outputs (return) come out the other side
Parameters are the empty slots/placeholders on a form (function definition), while arguments are the actual filled-in values you submit (when calling the function)
Imagine a vending machine - you insert money (arguments), make a selection (call function), and the item comes out through the output slot (return value). Without return, nothing comes out!

Picture a fishbowl - variables inside the function are like fish in the bowl (local scope). They can't swim out to the ocean (global scope). But the ocean water can be brought INTO the bowl (using global keyword)
Think of a lambda as a quick note or sticky slip - small, temporary, and meant for one-time use, unlike a full document (regular function)
*args is like a magic expandable backpack that can hold any number of items (positional). **kwargs is like a labeled suitcase where each item has a tag (keyword name)

analogies.analogy: Like writing a recipe card - you define the ingredients needed (parameters), the cooking steps (function body), and what dish comes out (return value). Once written, anyone can follow the recipe repeatedly
Like the buttons on a microwave - they define what settings you CAN use (power level, time). When you press them, you're passing arguments to get your food cooked
Like a vending machine return slot - it's the designated place where you receive what you asked for. If nothing is returned, you just get an empty-handed response (None)
Like a hotel's standard room amenities - unless you specifically request upgrades (pass custom arguments), everyone gets the basic setup (default values)

Like a private diary vs a public notice board - entries in your private diary (local variables) are only visible to you. Public notices (global variables) can be read by everyone but require special permission to modify from inside a private room (function)

Like ordering a quick coffee at a counter vs booking a full restaurant table - lambda is the quick counter order (simple, fast, one-line), regular function is the full dining experience (detailed, can have multiple courses/steps)
